evidence: holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows `goseg/system/` has 36 files while disk behavior is already split into focused subpackages (`goseg/system/disk/` and `goseg/system/storage/`), yet assigned disk shim tests remain in root-level `system` package (`disk_health_test.go`, `disk_shims_test.go`, `disk_storage_test.go`).

Assigned `goseg/system/disk_shims_test.go` validates wrappers that delegate to `goseg/system/storage` and `goseg/system/disk` adapters, indicating cross-folder seam behavior is owned by root package tests instead of colocated package tests.

holistic_context.structure.directory_profiles shows `goseg/shipworkflow/` has 49 files; assigned `lifecycle.go` holds generic stop/poll/persist helpers while `doc.go` describes a broad multi-domain workflow package, signaling continued flattening inside an already overloaded directory.
goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go compares timeout errors with `err == context.DeadlineExceeded` even though the injected poller interface allows wrapped timeout errors.
goseg/config/bootstrap.go uses package-level `initOnce` + `initErr`, so any transient initialization I/O failure is memoized and returned forever with no retry pathway.

Protocol validator tests in goseg/protocol/conformance/fixtures_test.go and goseg/protocol/contracts/family_specs_test.go assert generic non-nil or substring-based errors, while system/shipworkflow tests assert stable wrapping semantics with `errors.Is`.

findings.summary: Disk seam tests are anchored in root `system` package instead of the disk/storage package boundaries they verify.
evidence: `disk_shims_test.go` asserts behavior of root-level shim functions that immediately delegate to `system/storage` and `system/disk` concerns.

The directory profile for `goseg/system/` is large (36 files), while dedicated subdirectories already exist for disk adapters and storage implementations, so keeping seam tests in root increases boundary blur and navigation cost.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) create `goseg/system/diskshim/` (or `goseg/system/diskbridge/`) for root-wrapper ownership and move `disk_shims_test.go` plus wrapper functions there; 2) move storage-focused tests (`disk_storage_test.go`) under `goseg/system/storage/` and adapter-focused tests (`disk_health_test.go` cases that are adapter-specific) under `goseg/system/disk/`; 3) keep only orchestration-level integration tests in `goseg/system/`; 4) run import updates with `go test ./goseg/system/... ./goseg/system/disk/... ./goseg/system/storage/...` and `go test ./...` to validate no package-cycle or API breakage.

findings.summary: Generic lifecycle helpers remain in a 49-file workflow package instead of a narrower lifecycle boundary.
evidence: `lifecycle.go` contains reusable timeout/poll/persist primitives (`PollWithTimeout`, `PersistUrbitConfig`, `WaitForUrbitStop*`) that are not specific to one workflow branch.

The directory profile reports `goseg/shipworkflow/` at 49 files (23 production/26 test), which exceeds the mixed-concern threshold and increases discoverability friction when generic lifecycle code is kept in the same flat directory.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: 1) add `goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle/` (or `goseg/shiplifecycle/`) and move `lifecycle.go` + `lifecycle_test.go` there; 2) expose a small API from that package and update callers in `shipworkflow` runtime files to import it explicitly; 3) leave `doc.go` in root `shipworkflow` focused on orchestration ownership only; 4) run `go test ./goseg/shipworkflow/...` then `go test ./...` to validate imports and ensure move does not create cycles.

findings.summary: Protocol action identity is split across multiple packages with overlapping ownership boundaries.
evidence: `actionids.go` owns raw namespace/action constants (`upload`, `c2c`, `open-endpoint`, `connect`).
`protocol_contracts.go` rebuilds the same identity space as `ActionNamespace*`/`Action*` and formula-based `ContractID`s.
`fixtures_test.go` validates canonical bindings via `groundseg/protocol/actions` symbols instead of a single contract-identity source, indicating consumers depend on different identity packages.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 create `goseg/protocol/contracts/identity/` (or `goseg/protocol/identity/`) as the sole exported owner of namespace/action/contract-id bindings and move token declarations from `actionids` plus typed bindings from `protocol_contracts.go` into it. Stage 2 keep `goseg/protocol/contracts/` focused on catalog assembly/validation and switch conformance fixtures to import the new identity package only. Stage 3 add temporary aliases in old locations (`actionids` and any `actions` bridge) marked deprecated, then remove after downstream migration. Stage 4 run import updates and validation with `go test ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/conformance ./uploadsvc/...` and then `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Governance contract policy is scattered between README policy docs and inline runtime specs.
evidence: `README.md` declares backend quality-gate and governance documentation ownership.
Both `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` inline governance policy fields (`OwnerModule`, `Stability`, `SinceVersion`) directly in family specs.
`family_specs_test.go` constructs governance-bearing specs in tests, reinforcing that governance semantics are spread across runtime and test setup rather than a clearly isolated policy layer.
findings.suggestion: Staged reorg plan: Stage 1 introduce `goseg/protocol/contracts/governance/` with a dedicated schema and per-family policy declarations (protocol/startram) as the single policy source. Stage 2 move inline governance literals from `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` to that package and make catalog builders consume those declarations. Stage 3 narrow `family_specs_test.go` to validate builder behavior and add governance package tests for policy integrity/versioning rules. Stage 4 update docs links in `README.md` to the new package path and validate with `go test ./protocol/contracts ./protocol/conformance` plus `go test ./...`.

findings.summary: Timeout classification in ship lifecycle uses direct equality, so wrapped deadline errors can follow the wrong failure path.
evidence: goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle.go:62 checks `if err == context.DeadlineExceeded` on an error returned by injected `pollerFunc`.
The same function already wraps operational errors with `%w` (line 37), showing wrapped errors are expected in this flow.

Nearby tests in other modules use `errors.Is` for contract checks (goseg/system/disk_health_test.go:22,40; goseg/shipworkflow/lifecycle_test.go:19,29,63), creating inconsistent timeout/error matching behavior.
findings.suggestion: Change deadline handling to `errors.Is(err, context.DeadlineExceeded)` (or a dedicated timeout sentinel), and add a test where poller returns a wrapped deadline error to lock behavior.

findings.summary: Config bootstrap memoizes first initialization error indefinitely, making transient failures permanent and failure modes inconsistent with recoverable I/O operations.
evidence: goseg/config/bootstrap.go:41-46 stores first `initializeConfig()` result in package-level `initErr` guarded by `sync.Once`.
initializeConfig performs multiple external I/O steps (`openOrCreateConfigFile`, `persistConfig`, `ensureSessionKeyExists`, `UpdateConfigTyped`) where transient errors are possible (lines 59-95).
No bootstrap test covers retry-after-failure semantics; current tests in goseg/config/bootstrap_test.go only cover environment and runtime-mode helpers.
findings.suggestion: Split permanent validation errors from transient I/O errors and allow retry on transient failures (for example via guarded state machine or resettable initializer used by startup orchestration), then add tests for first-call failure followed by successful retry.

findings.summary: Protocol conformance tests validate failure presence/messages but not stable error identity, leaving validator error contracts inconsistent and fragile.
evidence: goseg/protocol/conformance/fixtures_test.go:35-37 only checks `err != nil` for unknown action fixture errors.
goseg/protocol/contracts/family_specs_test.go:71 and 95 rely on `strings.Contains(err.Error(), ...)` message matching.
By contrast, goseg/system/disk_health_test.go asserts wrapping semantics with `errors.Is`, indicating a stronger and more stable error contract style elsewhere.
findings.suggestion: Introduce exported sentinel/type errors for protocol validation failures (e.g., unknown action, unexpected ID, unknown deprecation target) and migrate tests to `errors.Is`/`errors.As` assertions instead of substring checks.

findings.summary: Disk storage test coverage includes a placeholder smoke test, so error propagation contracts are not consistently enforced across disk entry points.
evidence: goseg/system/disk_storage_test.go:5-8 contains only an unreachable placeholder (`if false`) and no behavioral assertions.
Other disk shim tests (goseg/system/disk_health_test.go) explicitly verify wrapped command/stat error propagation, demonstrating stronger expectations in sibling tests.
The mismatch means some disk wrappers may regress in error shape/context without failing tests.
findings.suggestion: Replace the placeholder with table-driven tests for key disk-storage wrappers and assert error wrapping/identity consistently (including command failures, parse failures, and filesystem failures).
